title: Sheep lice and keds: identification and treatment
subtitle: The external parasites that cause fleece damage and hide irritation.
excerpt: Sheep lice and keds are common in the UK, easiest to spot in autumn and winter before shearing, and controllable with a single pour-on treatment applied at the right time. Missed cases lead to fleece damage, weight loss, and anaemia. Most infestations enter the flock with bought-in animals.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb, ecto-parasite control guidance, ahdb.org.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep (2023). Moredun Research Institute, sheep ectoparasite factsheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: Melophagus ovinus, a wingless fly that lives permanently on the sheep, feeding on blood. Brown, leathery, and spider-like in appearance; around 5 mm long. Found by parting the fleece on the neck and belly. Females cement their pupae to wool fibres.

Damalinia ovis, a louse that feeds on skin scales and fleece fibres rather than blood. Causes intense irritation, fleece rubbing, and wool break. Seen as pale, slow-moving insects in the fleece when parted.

content.text: are both more visible and more treatable in the months following shearing, when the fleece is short and parasites are exposed. The traditional autumn-treatment window (October to November) catches the population before it builds up over winter in the dense fleece. Bought-in animals should be treated at quarantine before joining the main flock.
Part the fleece on the shoulder, neck, and belly and examine under good light.

are pale yellow, flat, and slow-moving; they cling to wool fibres and skin debris. Keds are larger, dark brown, and move quickly when exposed to light. Both cause scratching, fleece rubbing, and loss of condition. Heavily infested animals bite at their own flanks and rub against fences until the wool thins.

Pour-on macrolide products (ivermectin-based) are effective against keds and are licensed for sheep in the UK. Apply along the midline from poll to tail at the dose rate printed on the bottle for the animal's weight. For lice, organophosphate plunge dip or pour-on permethrin products are the licensed options. Check with your vet for current product availability and resistance patterns in your area, resistance to pour-on products has been confirmed in some Scottish lice populations.

Treat at shearing for keds (bare skin maximises product contact with the population). For lice, treat in autumn before the fleece thickens. Ked pupae attached to fleece are not killed by most treatments; a second application 10 to 14 days after the first is required to kill emerging adults before they reach reproductive age. Check the flock 3 weeks after treatment by parting the fleece, persisting live parasites suggest treatment failure and warrant a vet discussion.

Lice and keds transfer only by direct contact between animals. Quarantine all bought-in stock for at least three weeks and examine them before mixing with the flock. An animal with visible ked or louse infestation should be treated before quarantine ends, not after integration.
